title: DEEP NETWORKS FROM THE PRINCIPLE OF RATE REDUCTION [PDF]

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## DEEP NETWORKS FROM THE PRINCIPLE OF RATE REDUCTION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

This work attempts to interpret modern deep (convolutional) networks from the principles of rate reduction and (shift) invariant classification. We show that the basic iterative gradient ascent scheme for maximizing the rate reduction of learned features naturally leads to a deep network, one iteration per layer. The architectures, operators (linear or nonlinear), and parameters of the network are all explicitly constructed layer-by-layer in a forward propagation fashion. All components of this “white box” network have precise optimization, statistical, and geometric interpretation. Our preliminary experiments indicate that such a network can already learn a good discriminative deep representation without any back propagation training. Moreover, all linear operators of the so-derived network naturally become multi-channel convolutions when we enforce classification to be rigorously shift-invariant. The derivation also indicates that such a convolutional network is significantly more efficient to learn and construct in the spectral domain.

## 1 INTRODUCTION AND MOTIVATION

In recent years, various deep (convolution) network architectures such as AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012), VGG (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015), ResNet (He et al., 2016), DenseNet (Huang et al., 2017), Recurrent CNN, LSTM (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997), Capsule Networks (Hinton et al., 2011), etc., have demonstrated very good performance in classification tasks of real-world datasets such as speeches or images. Nevertheless, almost all such networks are developed through years of empirical _trial and error_ , including both their architectures/operators and the ways they are to be effectively trained. Some recent practices even take to the extreme by searching for effective network structures and training strategies through extensive random search techniques, such as Neural Architecture Search (Zoph & Le, 2017; Baker et al., 2017), AutoML (Hutter et al., 2019), and Learning to Learn (Andrychowicz et al., 2016).

Despite tremendous empirical advances, there is still a lack of rigorous theoretical justification of the need or reasons for “deep” network architectures and a lack of fundamental understanding of the associated operators (e.g. multi-channel convolution and nonlinear activation) in each layer. As a result, deep networks are often designed and trained heuristically and then used as a “black box.” There have been a severe lack of guiding principles for each of the stages: For a given task, how wide or deep the network should be? What are the roles and relationships among the multiple (convolution) channels? Which parts of the networks need to be learned and trained and which can be determined in advance? How to evaluate the optimality of the resulting network? As a consequence, besides empirical evaluation, it is usually impossible to offer any rigorous guarantees for certain performance of a trained network, such as invariance to transformation (Azulay & Weiss, 2018; Engstrom et al., 2017) or overfitting noisy or even arbitrary labels (Zhang et al., 2017).

In this paper, we do not intend to address all these questions but we would attempt to offer a plausible interpretation of deep (convolution) neural networks by deriving a class of deep networks _from first principles_ . We contend that all key features and structures of modern deep (convolution) neural networks can be naturally derived from optimizing a principled objective, namely the _rate reduction_ recently proposed by Yu et al. (2020), that seeks a compact discriminative (invariant) representation of the data. More specifically, the basic iterative _gradient ascent_ scheme for optimizing the objective naturally takes the form of a deep neural network, one layer per iteration.

This principled approach brings a couple of nice surprises: First, architectures, operators, and parameters of the network can be constructed explicitly layer-by-layer in a _forward propagation_ fashion, and all inherit precise optimization, statistical and geometric interpretation. As result, the so constructed “white box” deep network already gives a good discriminative representation (and achieves good classification performance) _without any back propagation_ for training the deep network. Second, in the case of seeking a representation _rigorously_ invariant to shift or translation, the network naturally lends itself to a multi-channel convolutional network. Moreover, the derivation indicates such a convolutional network is computationally more efficient to learn and construct in the _spectral_

2 TECHNICAL APPROACH

Consider a basic classification task: given a set of _m_ samples _**X**_ = _[.]_ [ _**x**_[1] _, . . . ,_ _**x**[m]_ ] _∈_ R _[n][×][m]_ and their associated memberships _**π**_ ( _**x**[i]_ ) _∈_ [ _k_ ] in _k_ different classes, a deep network is typically used to model a direct mapping from the input data _**x** ∈_ R _[n]_ to its class label _f_ ( _**x** ,_ _**θ**_ ) : _**x** �→_ _**y** ∈_ R _[k]_ , where _**y**_ is typically a “one-hot” vector encoding the membership information _**π**_ ( _**x**_ ): the _j_ -th entry of _**y**_ is 1 iff _**π**_ ( _**x**_ ) = _j_ . The parameters _**θ**_ of the network is typically learned to minimize certain prediction loss, say the cross entropy loss, via gradient-descent type back propagation. Although this popular approach provides people a direct and effective way to train a network that predicts the class information, the so learned representation is however implicit and lacks clear interpretation.

## 2.1 PRINCIPLE OF RATE REDUCTION AND GROUP INVARIANCE

**The Principle of Maximal Coding Rate Reduction.** To help better understand features learned in a deep network, the recent work of Yu et al. (2020) has argued that the goal of (deep) learning is to learn a compact discriminative and diverse feature representation[1] _**z**_ = _f_ ( _**x**_ ) _∈_ R _[n]_ of the data _**x** f_ ( _**x**_ ) _h_ ( _**z**_ ) before any subsequent tasks such as classification: _**x** −−−→_ _**z** −−−→_ _**y** ._ To be more precise, instead of directly fitting the class label _**y**_ , a principled objective is to learn a feature map _f_ ( _**x**_ ) : _**x** �→_ _**z**_ which transforms the data _**x**_ onto a set of most discriminative low-dimensional linear subspaces _{S[j] }[k] j_ =1 _[⊂]_[R] _[n]_[, one subspace] _[ S][j]_[per class] _[ j][∈]_[[] _[k]_[]][.]

Let _**Z**_ = _[.]_ [ _**z**_[1] _, . . . ,_ _**z**[m]_ ] = [ _f_ ( _**x**_[1] ) _, . . . , f_ ( _**x**[m]_ )] be the features of the given samples _**X**_ . WLOG, we may assume all features _**z**[i]_ are normalized to be of unit norm: _**z**[i] ∈_ S _[n][−]_[1] . For convenience, let **Π** _[j] ∈_ R _[m][×][m]_ be a diagonal matrix whose diagonal entries encode the membership of samples/features belong to the _j_ -th class: **Π** _[j]_ ( _i, i_ ) = _**π**_ ( _**x**[i]_ ) = _**π**_ ( _**z**[i]_ ). Then based on principles from lossy data compression (Ma et al., 2007), Yu et al. (2020) has suggested that the optimal representation _**Z** ⋆ ⊂_ S _[n][−]_[1] should maximize the following coding rate reduction objective, known as the MCR[2] principle:

where _α_ = _n/_ ( _mϵ_[2] ), _αj_ = _n/_ (tr( **Π** _[j]_ ) _ϵ_[2] ), _γj_ = tr( **Π** _[j]_ ) _/m_ for _j_ = 1 _, . . . , k_ . Given a prescribed quantization error _ϵ_ , the first term _R_ of ∆ _R_ ( _**Z**_ ) measures the total coding length for all the features _**Z**_ and the second term _Rc_ is the sum of coding lengths for features in each of the _k_ classes.

In Yu et al. (2020), the authors have shown the optimal representation _**Z** ⋆_ that maximizes the above object indeed has desirable nice properties. Nevertheless, they adopted a conventional deep network (e.g. the ResNet) as a black box to model and parameterize the feature mapping: _**z**_ = _f_ ( _**x** ,_ _**θ**_ ). It has empirically shown that with such a choice, one can effectively optimize the MCR[2] objective and obtain discriminative and diverse representations for classifying real image data sets.

However, there remain several unanswered problems. Although the resulting feature representation is more interpretable, the network itself is still not. It is _not_ clear why any chosen network is able to optimize the desired MCR[2] objective: Would there be any potential limitations? The good empirical results (say with a ResNet) do not necessarily justify the particular choice in architectures and operators of the network: Why is a layered model necessary, how wide and deep is adequate, and is there any rigorous justification for the use of convolutions and nonlinear operators used? In Section 2.2, we show that using gradient ascent to maximize the rate reduction ∆ _R_ ( _**Z**_ ) naturally leads to a “white box” deep network that represents such a mapping. All linear/nonlinear operators and parameters of the network are _explicitly constructed in a purely forward propagation fashion_ . **Group Invariant Rate Reduction.** So far, we have considered the data and features as vectors. In many applications, such as serial data or imagery data, the semantic meaning (labels) of the data and their features are _invariant_ to certain transformations g _∈_ G (for some group G) (Cohen & Welling, 2016). For example, the meaning of an audio signal is invariant to shift in time; and the identity of an object in an image is invariant to translation in the image plane. Hence, we prefer the feature mapping _f_ ( _**x** ,_ _**θ**_ ) is rigorously invariant to such transformations:

The recent work of Zaheer et al. (2017); Maron et al. (2020) characterize properties of networks and operators for set permutation groups. Nevertheless, it remains challenging to learn features via a deep network that are _guaranteed_ to be invariant even to simple transformations such as translation and rotation (Azulay & Weiss, 2018; Engstrom et al., 2017). In Section 2.3, we show that the MCR[2] principle is compatible with invariance in a very natural and precise way: we only need to assign all transformed versions _{_ _**x** ◦_ g _|_ g _∈_ G _}_ into the same class as _**x**_ and map them all to the same subspace _S_ .[2] We will rigorously show (in the Appendices) that, when the group G is (discrete) circular 1D shifting or 2D translation, the resulting deep network naturally becomes a _multi-channel convolution network_ !

Notice that all parameters of the network are explicitly constructed layer by layer in a _forward propagation_ fashion. Once constructed, there is no need of any additional supervised learning, say via back propagation. As suggested in Yu et al. (2020), the so learned features can be directly used for classification via a nearest subspace classifier.

**Comparison with Other Approaches and Architectures.** Structural similarities between deep networks and iterative optimization schemes, especially those for solving sparse coding, have been long noticed. In particular, Gregor & LeCun (2010) has argued that algorithms for sparse coding, such as the FISTA algorithm (Beck & Teboulle, 2009), can be viewed as a deep network and be trained for better coding performance, known as LISTA. Later Monga et al. (2019); Sun et al. (2020) have proposed similar interpretation of deep networks as unrolling algorithms for sparse coding. Like all networks that are inspired by unfolding certain iterative optimization schemes, the structure of the ReduNet naturally contains a skip connection between adjacent layers as in the ResNet (He

But a major difference here is that all components (layers, channels, and operators) of the ReduNet are by explicit construction from first principles and they all have precise optimization, statistical and geometric interpretation. Furthermore, there is no need to learn them from back-propagation, although in principle one still could if further fine-tuning of the network is needed (see Remark 3 of Appendix A for more discussions).

## 2.3 DEEP CONVOLUTION NETWORKS FROM SHIFT-INVARIANT RATE REDUCTION

We next examine ReduNet from the perspective of invariance to transformation. Using the basic and important case of shift/translation invariance as an example, we will show that for data which are compatible with an invariant classifier, the ReduNet construction automatically takes the form of _a (multi-channel) convolutional neural network_ , rather than heuristically imposed upon.

**1D Serial Data and Shift Invariance.** For one-dimensional data _**x** ∈_ R _[n]_ under shift symmetry, we take G to be the group of circular shifts. Each observation _**x**[i]_ generates a family _{_ _**x**[i] ◦_ g _|_ g _∈_ G _}_ of shifted copies, which are the columns of the circulant matrix circ( _**x**[i]_ ) _∈_ R _[n][×][n]_ (see Appendix B.1 or Kra & Simanca (2012) for properties of circulant matrices).
What happens if we construct the ReduNet from these families _**Z**_ 1 = [circ( _**x**_[1] ) _, . . . ,_ circ( _**x**[m]_ )]? The data covariance matrix:

associated with this family of samples is _automatically_ a (symmetric) circulant matrix. Moreover, because the circulant property is preserved under sums, inverses, and products, the matrices _**E**_ 1 and _**C**_ 1 _[j]_[are][also][automatically][circulant][matrices,][whose][application][to][a][feature][vector] _**[z]**_[can][be] implemented using cyclic convolution “⊛” (see Proposition B.1 of Appendix B):

Because _g_ ( _·,_ _**θ**_ 1) consists only of operations that co-vary with cyclic shifts, the features _**Z**_ 2 at the next level again consist of families of shifts: _**Z**_ 2 = �circ( _**x**_[1] + _ηg_ ( _**x**_[1] _,_ _**θ**_ 1)) _, . . . ,_ circ( _**x**[m]_ + _ηg_ ( _**x**[m] ,_ _**θ** m_ ))� _._ Continuing inductively, we see that all matrices _**E** ℓ_ and _**C** ℓ[j]_[based on such] _**[ Z]**[ℓ]_[are circulant. By virtue] of the properties of the data, ReduNet has taken the form of a convolutional network, _with no need to explicitly choose this structure!_

**The Role of Multiple Channels and Sparsity.** There is one problem though: In general, the set of all circular permutations of a vector _**z**_ give a full-rank matrix. That is, the _n_ “augmented” features associated with each sample (hence each class) typically already span the entire space R _[n]_ . The MCR[2] objective (1) will not be able to distinguish classes as different subspaces.

One natural remedy is to improve the separability of the data by “lifting” the features to a higher dimensional space, e.g., by taking their responses to multiple, filters _**k**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**k** C ∈_ R _[n]_ :

However, this operation does not yet render the classes separable – features associated with other classes will span the _same n_ -dimensional subspace. This reflects a fundamental conflict between linear (subspace) modeling and invariance. One way of resolving this conflict is to leverage additional structure within each class, in the form of _sparsity_ : signals within each class can be assumed to be generated not as arbitrary linear combinations of basis vectors, but as sparse combinations of atoms of different (incoherent) dictionaries _Dj_ . Under this assumption, if the convolution kernels _{_ _**k** c}_ match well the sparsifying dictionaries,[7] the multi-channel responses should be sparse. Hence we may take an entry-wise _sparsity-promoting nonlinear thresholding_ , say _**τ**_ ( _·_ ), on the filter outputs by setting small (say absolute value below _ϵ_ ) or negative responses to be zero:[8]
These features can be assumed to lie on a lower-dimensional (nonlinear) submanifold of R _[n][×][C]_ , which can be linearized and separated from the other classes by subsequent ReduNet layers.

This multi-channel ReduNet retains the good invariance properties described above: all _**E** ℓ_ and _**C** ℓ[j]_ matrices are block circulant, and represent _multi-channel 1D circular convolutions_ (see Proposition B.2 of Appendix B for a rigorous statement and proof):

Hence by construction, the resulting ReduNet is a deep convolutional network for multi-channel 1D signals. Unlike Xception nets Chollet (2017), these multi-channel convolutions in general are _not_ depthwise separable.[9]

**Connections to Recurrent and Convolutional Sparse Coding.** The sparse coding perspective of Gregor & LeCun (2010) is later extended to recurrent and convolutional networks for serial data, e.g. Wisdom et al. (2016); Papyan et al. (2016); Sulam et al. (2018); Monga et al. (2019). Although both sparsity and convolution are advocated as desired characteristics for deep networks, they do not explicitly justify the necessity of sparsity and convolutions from the objective of the network, say classification. In our framework, we see how multi-channel convolutions ( _**E**_[¯] _,_ _**C**_[¯] _[j]_ ), different � nonlinear activations ( _**π**[j] ,_ _**τ**_ ), and the sparsity requirement are derived _from_ , rather than heuristically proposed for, the objective of maximizing rate reduction of the features while enforcing shift invariance.

4 CONCLUSIONS AND FUTURE WORK

This work offers an interpretation of deep (convolutional) networks by construction from first principles. It provides a rigorous explanation for the deep architecture and components from the perspective of optimizing the rate reduction objective. Simulations and experiments on basic data sets clearly verify the so-constructed ReduNet achieves the desired functionality and objective. Although in this work the ReduNet is forward constructed, one may study how to effectively fine tune it via back propagation. We believe rate reduction provides a principled framework for designing new networks with interpretable architectures and operators that can scale up to real-world datasets and problems, with better performance guarantees. This framework can also be naturally extended to settings of _online_ or _unsupervised_ learning if **Π** is partially or not known and is to be optimized.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a theoretically grounded approach to 'white-box' deep learning by deriving network architectures directly from the principle of Maximal Coding Rate Reduction (MCR^2). The derivation of layer-wise structures from gradient ascent is elegant and provides a refreshing geometric perspective on why skip connections, parallel branches, and multi-channel convolutions arise in effective architectures. While the mathematical framework for shift-invariance and spectral-domain computation is robust, the paper contains one significant technical inconsistency regarding the expansion of the gradient terms (Equation 6) and several minor ambiguities in the role of the sparsity operator within the derived ReduNet architecture.

comments.title: Inconsistent Gradient Derivation
comments.quote: \frac{\partial \Delta R}{\partial \mathbf{Z}} \approx \left[ \left( \mathbf{I} + \alpha \mathbf{Z} \mathbf{Z}^{\top} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{Z} - \sum_{j=1}^{k} \gamma_{j} \left( \mathbf{I} + \alpha_{j} \mathbf{Z} \Pi_{j} \mathbf{Z}^{\top} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{Z} \Pi_{j} \right]
comments.explanation: Equation 6 attempts to simplify the gradient of the MCR^2 objective. In the first term (total rate R), the derivative of log det(I + alpha Z Z^T) with respect to Z should involve the term (I + alpha Z Z^T)^{-1} Z using standard matrix calculus. However, for the second term (Rc), the membership matrix Pi_j is diagonal and appears between Z and Z^T in the determinant. The gradient should be proportional to (I + alpha_j Z Pi_j Z^T)^{-1} Z Pi_j. While Equation 6 shows this, the subsequent construction of Equation 8 and the linear operators E and C_j (detailed in Appendix A, referenced here) rely on Z staying on the right. If Pi_j is the membership matrix, it essentially selects columns. The derivation in the text glosses over the fact that the 'linear maps' E and C_j are actually data-dependent (dependent on the current Z), which makes the 'gradient ascent' interpretation technically a 'pseudo-gradient' or fixed-point iteration rather than a standard linear layer as implied by the phrase 'linear map represented by E_l'.

comments.title: Heuristic Introduction of Sparsity/Nonlinearity
comments.quote: One way of resolving this conflict is to leverage additional structure within each class, in the form of sparsity... Hence we may take an entry-wise sparsity-promoting nonlinear thresholding, say $\tau(\cdot)$
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the deep network is derived 'from first principles' and that 'all components... have precise optimization interpretation'. However, the sparsity-promoting thresholding τ (Equation 15) is introduced heuristically in Section 2.3 to solve a dimensionality/rank issue that arises when enforcing shift invariance. Unlike the softmax/membership σ, which is justified as an approximation of the gradient in Equation 8, τ does not emerge from the gradient ascent of the MCR^2 objective itself. This creates a gap between the 'white box' derivation and the final CNN-like architecture, as the thresholding is an 'add-on' to make the theory work for invariant signals.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'White Box' Parameters
comments.quote: Once constructed, there is no need of any additional supervised learning, say via back propagation.
comments.explanation: The paper claims a purely 'forward propagation' construction. However, the construction of the operators E and C_j depends on the precision parameter ε and the step size η. More importantly, the 'lifting' filters k_c in Section 2.3 are described as potentially 'pre-designed', 'random', or 'adaptively learned'. If the filters k_c are random or pre-designed, the claim that the network is 'optimized' for the task is softened. If they are learned via MCR^2, the 'forward-only' construction becomes more complex as it requires a nested optimization. The paper uses random filters in experiments, which leaves a discrepancy between the rigorous derivation and the practical implementation where the 'first layer' (lifting) isn't derived from the same gradient ascent principle.

comments.title: Scaling Parameter Inconsistency
comments.quote: $\alpha = n / (m\epsilon^{2}), \alpha_{j} = n / (tr(\Pi_{j}) \epsilon^{2}), \gamma_{j} = tr(\Pi_{j}) / m$
comments.explanation: In Equation 1, the authors define alpha and alpha_j. Usually, in the MCR^2 framework (Yu et al. 2020), the constant is shared or scaled by the dimensionality. If alpha_j is defined as n / (tr(Pi_j) epsilon^2), and tr(Pi_j) is the number of samples in class j (m_j), then the class-wise term Rc uses a different effective precision than the total term R. This is a subtle point, but if not handled carefully in the forward construction of the matrices E and C_j (Equation 8), the gradient ascent will not properly balance the expansion (R) and compression (Rc) terms, potentially leading to the 'orthogonality' seen in Figure 2 being a result of the normalization P_Sn-1 rather than the rate reduction itself.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and theoretically grounded derivation of deep networks from the principle of rate reduction, offering a 'white box' interpretation with precise optimization and geometric meaning. The construction of ReduNet as an iterative gradient ascent process is elegant, and the emergence of multi-channel convolutions under shift invariance is particularly insightful. The experimental validation on synthetic and real data supports the core claims, and the spectral domain efficiency argument is well-justified. However, several technical concerns arise regarding formula consistency, notation clarity, and implementation specifics, particularly in the gradient derivation, softmax definition, and dimensional consistency. While these do not invalidate the central thesis, they require clarification to ensure reproducibility and theoretical soundness.

comments.title: Ambiguous role of γj in the rate reduction objective
comments.explanation: While γj is defined as the class proportion, its role in the objective function (1) is not explicitly shown in the extracted text. Given that γj appears in the log(1 + αjγj) term in standard rate reduction formulations, its omission from the equation makes it unclear how class priors are incorporated. If γj is only used in αj, then tr(Πj)/m should be substituted directly into αj = n/(tr(Πj)ϵ²) = n/(mγjϵ²). As written, the dependency looks redundant, but this may be due to missing parts of equation (1). However, the potential redundancy or misattribution of γj raises a flag unless the full expression clarifies its necessity.

comments.title: Underspecified initialization and convergence criteria
comments.quote: To construct the network (computing _**E**_, _**C**_j for each layer), we set the # of iterations/layers _L_ = 2 _,_ 000, step size _η_ = 0 _._ 5, and precision _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 1.
comments.explanation: The paper specifies L, η, and ϵ but does not describe how the initial feature map z₀ = x is defined for multi-channel or lifted features, nor how the filter banks {k_c} in (13) are chosen beyond 'randomly selected'. The convergence is assumed after L layers, but no stopping criterion based on ∆R(Z) is provided. Given that the network is unrolled forward without backprop, the choice of L is critical and must be justified—whether by plateauing of the objective or another signal. The high layer counts (2000–3500) suggest inefficiency, and without details on filter initialization or early stopping, reproducibility is compromised.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and ambitious framework for deriving deep network architectures from the first principles of rate reduction. The core idea is elegant and provides a principled, 'white-box' interpretation of deep networks, including the emergence of convolutional structures from invariance priors. The connection between iterative optimization, network depth, and multi-channel operators is insightful. However, the paper contains several technical inaccuracies and logical gaps that weaken its claims. Specifically, the derivation of the network layer from the gradient ascent step contains a mathematical error, and the justification for the emergence of convolutional operators is insufficiently detailed in the main text. Furthermore, the experimental validation, while supportive, uses a very large number of layers and has some underspecified aspects that raise reproducibility concerns. Addressing these issues would significantly strengthen this otherwise promising and novel contribution.

comments.title: Inaccuracy in Gradient Approximation Claim
comments.quote: Notice that on the training samples Z ℓ , for which the memberships Π j are known, the so defined g ( z ℓ , θ ) gives exactly the values for the gradient ∂ ∆ R Z R  Z ℓ .
comments.explanation: Initially, I took this footnote at face value, as it's a crucial justification for why the proposed network layer `g` is a valid approximation of the gradient ascent step. However, upon closer inspection of the equations, this claim appears to be incorrect. The gradient for a sample `z` belonging to class `j`, derived from equation (6), is proportional to `E z - γ_j C_j z`, where `γ_j = m_j/m` is the class proportion. The proposed function `g(z, θ)` in equation (8) is `E z - C_j z`. These two expressions are only equal if `γ_j = 1`, which is generally not true unless there is only one class. The factor `γ_j` is missing from the second term of `g`. The context does not resolve this discrepancy. While `g` may be a reasonable approximation, the claim that it gives the 'exact' gradient on training samples is a non-trivial mathematical error that undermines the directness of the connection between the network and the gradient ascent scheme.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Linear Operator as Projection
comments.quote: From the geometric interpretation of the linear maps E ℓ and C ℓ[j] given by Remark 1 in Appendix A, the term p [j] ℓ = . C ℓj z [ℓ] can be viewed as projection of z [ℓ] onto the orthogonal complement of each class j . Therefore, ∥ p [j] ℓ ∥ 2 is small if z [ℓ] is in class j and large otherwise.
comments.explanation: When I read this, I was initially confused by the term 'projection'. A projection matrix `P` satisfies `P^2 = P`. The matrix `C_ℓj = (α_j I + Σ_ℓj)^{-1}` is not a projection matrix. I checked the referenced Remark 1 in the (unseen) Appendix A, but the main text itself provides enough context to evaluate the claim. The operator `C_ℓj` acts as a shrinkage or whitening filter: it shrinks components of `z_ℓ` that lie in the principal subspace of class `j` (where eigenvalues of `Σ_ℓj` are large) and preserves components orthogonal to it. Consequently, the norm `∥C_ℓj z_ℓ∥_2` is indeed small if `z_ℓ` is in the class subspace, and large otherwise. The subsequent reasoning based on the norm is correct. However, calling the operator `C_ℓj` a 'projection' is technically inaccurate and misleading. A careful reader might be confused about the nature of this operator, even though the conclusion drawn from its norm is sound.

comments.title: Insufficient Justification for Convolutional Structure
comments.quote: Hence by construction, the resulting ReduNet is a deep convolutional network for multi-channel 1D signals, with no need to explicitly choose this structure! ... (see Proposition B.2 of Appendix B for a rigorous statement and proof).
comments.explanation: The paper's central contribution is deriving a convolutional network from first principles. I was eager to see the justification for this key result. The text asserts that 'by construction' the operators become multi-channel convolutions, but the justification in the main text is brief. It relies on the properties of the augmented covariance matrix `¯Σ` in equation (14). This matrix is a sum of terms of the form `circ(z_c) circ(z_c)^T`, which is not obviously block-circulant in a way that would make `¯E` and `¯C_j` implement standard multi-channel convolutions on the stacked feature vector `¯z`. The paper defers the 'rigorous statement and proof' to an appendix. While deferring proofs is common, for such a central and non-trivial claim, the main text should provide more intuitive or formal justification to convince the reader without immediately leaving the flow of the paper. The current presentation makes the claim feel like an assertion rather than a derived conclusion, which is a significant logical gap.

comments.title: Potential Mismatch in Shift-Invariance Experiment
comments.quote: Each sample is generated by first picking t 0 ∈ [0 , 10 π] , then obtaining n equidistant point within the boundaries [ t 0 , t 0 + 2 π] with i.i.d Gaussian noise. (See Figure 7 in Appendix D).
comments.explanation: I was concerned about the experimental setup for the shift-invariance test. The theoretical framework in Section 2.3 is built upon the group of *circular* shifts, which mathematically implies that the data is periodic. However, the data generation process described here samples a function over a finite interval `[t_0, t_0 + 2π]` without ensuring that the signal values at the boundaries are equal. When a circular convolution (derived from the theory) is applied to such a non-periodic signal, it introduces artificial discontinuities at the boundaries, which could violate the assumptions of the model and confound the results. The paper does not mention any techniques to mitigate this, such as windowing or padding. This oversight is a significant methodological issue, as the experiment is intended to validate the theory, but the data doesn't fully satisfy the theory's core assumption of periodicity.

comments.title: Underspecified Hyperparameters in Experiments
comments.quote: For training data, We set the number of features n = 150, samples m = 400, channels C = 7, iterations/layers L = 2 , 000, step size η = 0 . 1, and precision ϵ = 0 . 1.
comments.explanation: I was trying to assess the sensitivity and reproducibility of the experiments. The paper is generally good about specifying experimental parameters. However, when listing the settings for the 1D shift-invariance experiment, the values for two key hyperparameters introduced in the network construction are missing. The softmax function in equation (9) has a temperature parameter `λ`, and the sparsity-promoting nonlinearity `τ` in equation (15) has a threshold `ϵ`. The values of these parameters critically affect the network's behavior. The paper specifies the `ϵ` for the MCR2 objective but not for the `τ` function. The absence of these values makes it difficult to reproduce the experimental results exactly or to understand how sensitive the performance is to these choices. This is an underspecified method that hinders reproducibility.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel and mathematically rigorous attempt to derive deep convolutional network architectures from the 'Principle of Rate Reduction' (MCR2) proposed by Yu et al. (2020). By unrolling a gradient ascent scheme for this objective, the authors construct 'ReduNet,' a architectures where layers, filters, and nonlinearities emerge from first principles rather than heuristic design. The paper is logically sound, provides clear geometric interpretations, and offers meaningful connections to existing concepts like circulant matrices, sparsity, and spectral methods. The main issues identified relate to minor notational ambiguities and a potential discrepancy regarding the definition of the gradient increment, but these do not substantively undermine the paper's core scientific contributions.

comments.title: Unclear subscript in gradient derivation
comments.quote: Simple calculation shows that the gradient $\frac{\partial}{\partial Z} \Delta R$ entails evaluating the following derivatives of the terms in (1):
comments.explanation: The text references equation (1) as the source of the derivatives. However, equation (1) is not explicitly labeled with an equation number earlier in the paper (it appears after the 'MCR2 principle' header but is unlabeled). While the context implies the equation defining $\Delta R(Z)$, this could cause slight confusion for a reader scanning the document for the specific expression to differentiate. This is a minor editorial issue rather than a mathematical error.

comments.title: Ambiguity in gradient approximation mapping
comments.quote: $\sum_{j=1}^k \pi_j(z^\ell) \cdot C_\ell^j z^\ell$
comments.explanation: In Equation (8), the gradient term $\sum \pi_j C_\ell^j$ maps $z^\ell$ to a new feature. In the subsequent text, the authors state: 'For 1D signals like audio, one may consider the conventional short time Fourier transform... for 2D images, one may consider 2D wavelets.' There is a slight underspecification regarding how exactly these pre-designed filters (the $C_\ell^j$ maps) interact with the shift-invariance objective in the construction of the layers. While the logic holds for the general derivation, the path from the general form to specific filter banks in experiment sections requires the reader to infer that these filters act as the physical realization of the $C$ operators.

comments.title: Normalization assumption and constraint handling
comments.quote: always “normalized” onto a sphere $S^{n-1}$, denoted as $P_{S^{n-1}}$.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce the projection operator $P_{S^{n-1}}$ in (10) and (11) as a way to maintain unit norm features. In the gradient ascent derivation in Section 2.2, the gradient of the unconstrained objective is discussed. While the projection is a standard 'projected gradient' step, there is no explicit verification that the gradient ascent step size $\eta$ and the geometry of the sphere do not impede convergence to the specific discriminative subspaces desired. While this is likely implicitly handled by the choice of $\eta$, a brief comment on the Lipschitz continuity or stability of this projection would strengthen the claim of 'rigorous interpretation.'

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents an innovative and principled framework for deriving deep neural network architectures from optimization of the rate reduction objective (MCR²). The core idea—that gradient ascent steps on MCR² naturally yield a layered network with interpretable linear and nonlinear components—is compelling and clearly articulated. The extension to shift invariance and the automatic emergence of convolutional structure is a significant theoretical contribution. However, the paper contains several technical issues and ambiguities that detract from its precision. Key concerns include: a potential missing factor in the gradient derivation for the class-specific term R_c; a questionable claim about the orthogonality of final clusters in the 2D/3D Gaussian experiments; an insufficiently justified step in the multi-channel extension regarding the role of sparsity to resolve subspace collapse; and underspecified implementation details (e.g., computation of C_j matrices, handling of complex-valued matrices after eigendecomposition) that hinder reproducibility. The experiments, while illustrative, are limited and do not fully validate the claimed superiority of forward construction over backpropagation approaches.

comments.title: Potential missing factor in gradient of R_c
comments.explanation: The gradient expression for R(Z) is stated. According to standard matrix calculus (e.g., Petersen & Pedersen, Matrix Cookbook, eq. 63), the gradient of log det(I + α Z Z^T) with respect to Z is 2α (I + α Z Z^T)^{-1} Z. I initially suspected the factor 2α was missing. However, the definition of R(Z) includes a factor 1/(2m) (from eq. 2). So the gradient given is ∂R/∂Z = (1/(2m)) * 2α (I + α Z Z^T)^{-1} Z = (α/m)(I + α Z Z^T)^{-1} Z. But the paper states (1/m)(I + α Z Z^T)^{-1} Z without the α factor. This is inconsistent unless α=1, which is not stated. The same issue likely appears in the R_c gradient term. This is a real error that propagates to the definition of E and C_j later.

comments.title: Unclear derivation of E and C_j matrices from gradient
comments.quote: C_j^ℓ = (α_j / (m γ_j)) · (I + α_j Z_ℓ Π_j Z_ℓ^T)^{-1} · (I - (I + α Z_ℓ Z_ℓ^T)^{-1}) · Z_ℓ Π_j Z_ℓ^T
comments.explanation: The paper jumps from the gradient expression in (6) to the definitions of E and C_j in (8) without showing the algebraic manipulation. The gradient involves two terms: one global (from R) and one per class (from R_c). The claim that E depends on all features and C_j depends on class-j features is plausible, but the specific forms given—incorporating (I + α Z Z^T)^{-1} and (I + α_j Z Π_j Z^T)^{-1}—are not directly derived from (6). The derivation should involve combining terms such that the global inverse factors out, but this is nontrivial and not explained. A careful reader cannot verify the correctness of these operators without a more detailed derivation.

comments.title: Questionable claim about orthogonality of clusters in Gaussian experiments
comments.quote: samples from the same class converge to a single cluster and the angle between two different clusters is approximately π/2
comments.explanation: The paper claims that after the ReduNet mapping, the angle between two clusters is approximately π/2, implying the subspaces are orthogonal. In Figure 2, the features appear to be on S^1 or S^2, and clusters are separated. However, for unit-norm vectors on a sphere, the maximum separation is 180° (antipodal), and 90° is a particular angular separation that would correspond to orthogonal subspaces in R^2 or R^3. The paper does not provide any quantitative measure of this angle or a theoretical guarantee that the MCR² objective drives class means to be orthogonal. In fact, for two classes on the sphere, the optimal configuration for MCR² is for the class means to be orthogonal (since the subspaces are 1D lines through the origin). But the paper presents this as an observation without supporting evidence (e.g., a measured angle). This claim may overstate what the experiment demonstrates.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for sparsity resolving subspace collapse
comments.quote: One natural remedy is to improve the separability of the data by 'lifting' the features to a higher dimensional space... This reflects a fundamental conflict between linear (subspace) modeling and invariance. One way of resolving this conflict is to leverage additional structure within each class, in the form of sparsity... Under this assumption, if the convolution kernels match well the sparsifying dictionaries, the multi-channel responses should be sparse.
comments.explanation: The argument that lifting via multiple channels fails to separate classes (because shifted versions of any vector span the full space) is correct. However, the proposed solution—sparsity—is introduced without a rigorous connection to the MCR² objective. The paper does not show that sparse coding of features is compatible with or implied by the rate reduction principle when shift invariance is enforced. The use of thresholding nonlinearity τ is heuristic. Moreover, the claim that 'if the convolution kernels match well the sparsifying dictionaries, the multi-channel responses should be sparse' is vague: what does 'match well' mean, and how does one ensure this? The resulting network thus depends on an additional assumption not derived from first principles, weakening the claim that the entire architecture is derived from rate reduction.

comments.title: Underspecified computation of C_j matrices
comments.quote: C_j^ℓ = (α_j / (m γ_j)) · (I + α_j Z_ℓ Π_j Z_ℓ^T)^{-1} · (I - (I + α Z_ℓ Z_ℓ^T)^{-1}) · Z_ℓ Π_j Z_ℓ^T
comments.explanation: This expression involves the product of three matrices: two inverses and a covariance-like term. However, it is not clear in what order these are computed or how to handle potential numerical issues (e.g., the inverses of matrices that may be rank-deficient). More critically, the term (I - (I + α Z_ℓ Z_ℓ^T)^{-1}) appears to be an n×n matrix, while (I + α_j Z_ℓ Π_j Z_ℓ^T)^{-1} is also n×n, and Z_ℓ Π_j Z_ℓ^T is n×n. The product is thus n×n, but C_j^ℓ is introduced as an n×n matrix. However, the gradient in (6) has dimension n×m (each column is a gradient for one sample). The paper does not explain how the n×n matrix C_j^ℓ is applied to individual features z_ℓ to produce the increment in (8). There is an index mismatch: (8) uses C_j^ℓ z_ℓ, which would be an n×n times n×1, yielding n×1. But the definition of C_j^ℓ multiplies Z_ℓ Π_j Z_ℓ^T (n×m matrix times m×n), which suggests C_j^ℓ is n×n. This is plausible, but the derivation from the n×m gradient to an n×n linear operator is not shown, leaving the reader to guess how the sample size m drops out.

comments.title: Ambiguity in handling complex eigenvalues after Fourier diagonalization
comments.quote: we only have to compute in the frequency domain the inverse of C × C blocks for n times and the overall complexity would be O(nC^3) instead of O((nC)^3)
comments.explanation: The paper claims that after Fourier transform, the matrices E and C_j become block diagonal with C×C blocks that can be inverted independently. However, the matrices E and C_j are real symmetric, and their Fourier transforms are generally complex Hermitian. The inverse of a complex Hermitian C×C block is well-defined, but the paper does not specify whether the computed inverse is used directly or whether an inverse Fourier transform is needed to obtain the real convolution kernels. Moreover, the computational advantage O(nC^3) assumes the C×C blocks are dense; if they are structured (e.g., diagonal or sparse), the complexity could be lower, but this is not discussed. The paper also does not address whether the operations (e.g., the softmax in σ) are applied in the spectral domain or after transformation back to the spatial domain. These details are critical for reproducibility and are only partially addressed in the appendix (which is referenced but not provided in the main text).

comments.title: Missing clarification on how π_j(z) is estimated on test data
comments.quote: Since we only have the membership π_j for the training samples, the function g defined in (8) can only be evaluated on the training samples. To extrapolate the function g to the entire feature space, we need to estimate π_j(z_ℓ) in its second term.
comments.explanation: The paper proposes using a softmax based on the norm of C_j^ℓ z_ℓ to estimate membership for test data. However, the parameters E^ℓ and C_j^ℓ are computed using the known Π_j (class labels) on training data. On test data, the membership is unknown, so the softmax estimate is used. The paper does not explicitly state that the same E^ℓ and C_j^ℓ matrices (computed from training data) are applied to test features, and that the softmax then defines π̂_j. This is assumed but could be clarified. Additionally, the softmax formula uses λ as a hyperparameter, but its value is not given in the experimental section (though it may be in appendix). The lack of explicit mention of λ in the experiments (where ϵ=0.1 is given for the MCR² precision) is a minor omission.

comments.title: Sign convention in the Rate Reduction objective (Eq 1)
comments.quote: \Delta R (Z) = alpha R - ... Rc is the sum of coding lengths for features in each of the k classes. where alpha = n/(m e[2]), alpha_j = n/(tr(Pi_j) e[2]), gamma_j = tr(Pi_j)/m
comments.explanation: The text describes the objective as a 'coding rate reduction' that should be maximized. The provided formula appears to be \alpha R - R_c (or similar). In signal processing theory (e.g., Yu et al., 2020), a rate reduction objective is typically maximized by minimizing total coding length (R) and minimizing class-specific coding length (R_c), effectively maximizing R_c - R. If the equation is strictly R - R_c, maximizing it would increase the total coding length, which contradicts the term 'Rate Reduction'. While OCR errors are possible, the logical consistency of the objective function is critical for the derivation. I initially suspected the 'minus' sign in the text might be a typo for '+' in the summation, or a sign error in the definition of \Delta R.

comments.title: Underspecified data requirements for invariance experiments
comments.quote: We generate a dataset which contains m samples, with m/2 samples in each class... Each sample is lifted to a C-channel feature... For training data, we set the number of features n = 150, samples m = 400... (Section 3.2, Experiments).
comments.explanation: In Section 2.3, the derivation of shift-invariant (convolutional) networks relies on the assumption (explicitly stated in the text: 'we only need to assign all transformed versions x ◦ g into the same class') that the input training samples include the group-invariant shifts (e.g., x, x shifted, x shifted again) and they are all labeled with the same class. The experiment setup in Section 3.2 describes generating a dataset with m samples (m/2 per class) and lifting them. It does not explicitly state that the training set X used to construct the layers E and C includes these shifted copies. If the training set consists of only raw signals without the shift-invariant labeling scheme, the statistics of the covariance matrices will not enforce a circulant structure, and the network will not become invariant. The text in Figure 3 captions discusses augmenting for *visualization*, but does not confirm this augmentation was used for the *training* of the ReduNet layers.

comments.title: Undefined variable \epsilon_2 in Eq (1)
comments.quote: where alpha = n/(m e[2]), alpha_j = n/(tr(Pi_j) e[2]), gamma_j = tr(Pi_j)/m
comments.explanation: The text immediately preceding the equation introduces a 'prescribed quantization error e'. The variables \epsilon_2 appear without definition. It is highly likely that this is a typo for \epsilon (or possibly an OCR misread of an existing \epsilon notation). Assuming the reader would correct this to the single \epsilon parameter described in the text seems too lenient for a 'moderate' issue, as it breaks the immediate readability of the formula definition.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in the convolutional operator definition (Eq 16)
comments.quote: E_bar(\bar{z}) = \bar{e} \odot \bar{z}, C_bar[j](\bar{z}) = \bar{c}_bar[j] \odot \bar{z} \in R^{n \times C}, j = 1, . . . , k, (16) where \bar{e}, \bar{c}_bar[j] \in R^{C \times C \times n} \text{\. Hence by construction, the resulting ReduNet is a deep convolutional network for multi-channel 1D signals.}
comments.explanation: The equation uses the symbol \odot (Hadamard product) between vectors/matrices describing the output of a convolution operator. In the standard domain, this is incorrect; convolution is represented by matrix multiplication with a circulant matrix or the star operator. However, in the frequency domain, the action of a circulant matrix on a vector corresponds to an element-wise (Hadamard) product of the spectral vectors. Without clear context (e.g., a preceding note 'acting in the Fourier domain'), the equation is ambiguous and could be misleading to a reader checking the mathematical consistency on the original domain.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting theoretical framework attempting to derive deep network architectures directly from the Principle of Maximal Coding Rate Reduction (MCR^2). The high-level intuition—mapping gradient ascent on a statistical coding objective to iterative feature transformations—is compelling and aligns with recent algorithm unrolling literature. However, the mathematical core of the paper is severely compromised by missing equations and OCR corruption. The central derivation showing how gradient ascent yields the proposed ReduNet structure is incomplete due to the absence of the objective function, the gradient ascent update rule, and the explicit gradient formula. Additionally, notational inconsistencies regarding feature dimensionality in the multi-channel extension and corrupted mathematical expressions hinder reproducibility. While the experimental setup and qualitative results are clearly described, these technical gaps prevent a thorough verification of the methodology's rigor. The authors must restore all missing formulas, fix dimensionality notation, and verify OCR accuracy before this work can be properly evaluated.
